title: He reads every book first
summary: A second-hand book stall owner in Cape Town will not sell a book until he has read it.
body:
There is a small book stall on Long Street in Cape Town. It is between a coffee shop and a barber. The stall has three tables and many wooden boxes. The boxes hold second-hand books.

The owner is Mr Khaya. He is fifty-five. He started the stall in 2003. He does not have a sign. People know it.

Mr Khaya does something strange. He reads every book before he sells it.

Yes, every book. If a customer brings him fifty books to sell, he takes them home. He reads them. Some take one evening. Some take a week. Only then does he put them in his boxes.

A woman asked him last year. She said, 'But why? It is only a book stall. People want to buy quickly.'

Mr Khaya said, 'Books are not bread. A book is a person. If I sell you a person you do not know, that is not fair. I want to be able to tell you about every book. I want to say: this one is funny in the middle. This one is sad. This one is short but heavy. This one is for a rainy Saturday.'

He sells fewer books than other shops. But his customers come back. Some come every month. They sit at the small table. He brings them coffee. He says, 'I have one for you. I read it on Tuesday. I thought of you.'

That is how he sells books.

annotations.meaningVi: đồ cũ / qua sử dụng (tính từ ghép)
nặng (ở đây: nặng về cảm xúc)
ít hơn (đếm được, so sánh hơn)

grammarNotes.title: fewer vs less
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Fewer' đi với danh từ đếm được (books, people, days). 'Less' đi với danh từ không đếm được (water, time, money). Trong bài: 'He sells fewer books than other shops.' Sách đếm được → fewer. Không nói 'less books' trong văn viết chuẩn.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Fewer' is used with countable nouns (books, people, days). 'Less' is used with uncountable nouns (water, time, money). In the passage: 'He sells fewer books than other shops.' Books are countable, so we use fewer. 'Less books' is non-standard in careful writing.

translationVi:
Có một sạp sách nhỏ trên đường Long Street ở Cape Town. Nó nằm giữa một quán cà phê và một tiệm cắt tóc. Sạp có ba cái bàn và nhiều thùng gỗ. Trong thùng là sách cũ.

Chủ sạp là ông Khaya. Ông năm mươi lăm tuổi. Ông mở sạp năm 2003. Ông không có biển hiệu. Người ta vẫn biết.

Ông Khaya làm một việc lạ. Ông đọc hết mỗi cuốn sách trước khi bán.

Đúng, mỗi cuốn. Nếu một khách mang đến năm mươi cuốn để bán, ông mang về nhà. Ông đọc. Có cuốn mất một tối. Có cuốn mất một tuần. Sau đó ông mới xếp vào thùng.

Năm ngoái có một bà hỏi ông. Bà nói: 'Sao vậy? Chỉ là sạp sách thôi mà. Người ta muốn mua nhanh chứ.'

Ông Khaya nói: 'Sách không phải bánh mì. Một cuốn sách là một con người. Nếu tôi bán cho cô một con người mà tôi không biết, vậy không công bằng. Tôi muốn nói được cho cô nghe về từng cuốn. Tôi muốn nói: cuốn này buồn cười ở giữa. Cuốn này buồn. Cuốn này ngắn mà nặng. Cuốn này hợp với một thứ Bảy có mưa.'

Ông bán ít sách hơn các tiệm khác. Nhưng khách quay lại. Có người tháng nào cũng tới. Họ ngồi ở cái bàn nhỏ. Ông pha cà phê. Ông nói: 'Tôi có một cuốn cho chị. Thứ Ba tôi đọc xong. Tôi nghĩ đến chị.'

Ông bán sách như thế đó.

options: On Long Street in Cape Town, between a coffee shop and a barber
questions.explanation: First paragraph describes the exact location.

questions.prompt: When did Mr Khaya start the stall?
What strange thing does he do?
options: He reads every book before he sells it
He sells books only at night
He gives books away for free
He only sells books he wrote himself
questions.explanation: 'He reads every book before he sells it.'

questions.prompt: What does he do with fifty books a customer brings him?
options: He takes them home and reads them
He puts them straight in the boxes
He throws away half of them
He sends them to another shop
questions.explanation: 'He takes them home. He reads them.'

questions.prompt: Why does he say 'a book is a person'?
options: Because selling a book is like introducing someone — you must know them first
Because each book is shaped like a body
Because he meets each author personally
Because the books belong to his neighbours
questions.explanation: His quoted reasoning to the woman.

questions.prompt: Which description does he give as an example?
questions.explanation: Listed in his quote among the four descriptions.

questions.prompt: Does he sell more or fewer books than other shops?
questions.explanation: 'He sells fewer books than other shops.'

questions.prompt: What does he offer customers who come back?
options: Coffee, and a book chosen with them in mind
questions.explanation: 'He brings them coffee… I thought of you.'

options: Slow, personal selling can create loyalty that speed cannot.
Long Street is the best street in Cape Town.
questions.explanation: His method is slow but his customers come back.

blanks.explanation: Set on Long Street in Cape Town.
Some take one evening, some a week.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mr Khaya says, 'A book is a person.' If you were to recommend ONE book you have read to a friend you care about, which book would it be — and what would you tell that friend about it in one sentence?
